papers.title: INFERNO: Galactic winds in dwarf galaxies with star-by-star simulations including runaway stars
papers.author_names: Eric P. Andersson Oscar Agertz Florent Renaud Romain Teyssier

abstract.text: The formation and evolution of galaxies have proved sensitive to the inclusion of stellar feedback, which is therefore crucial to any successful galaxy model. We present INFERNO, a new model for hydrodynamic simulations of galaxies, which incorporates resolved stellar objects with star-by-star calculations of when and where the injection of enriched material, momentum, and energy takes place. INFERNO treats early stellar kinematics to include phenomena such as walkaway and runaway stars. We employ this innovative model on simulations of a dwarf galaxy and demonstrate that our physically motivated stellar feedback model can drive vigorous galactic winds. This is quantified by mass and metal loading factors in the range of $10-100$, and an energy loading factor close to unity. Outflows are established close to the disc, are highly multi-phase, spanning almost $8$ orders of magnitude in temperature, and with a clear dichotomy between mass ejected in cold, slow-moving ($T\\\\lesssim5\\\\times10^4\\\\,{\\\\rm K}$, $v<100\\\\,{\\\\rm km\\\\,s}^{-1}$) gas and energy ejected in hot, fast-moving ($T>10^6\\\\,{\\\\rm K}$, $v>100\\\\,{\\\\rm km\\\\,s}^{-1}$) gas. In contrast to massive disc galaxies, we find a surprisingly weak impact of the early stellar kinematics, with runaway stars having little to no effect on our results, despite exploding in diffuse gas outside the dense star-forming gas, as well as outside the galactic disc entirely. We demonstrate that this weak impact in dwarf galaxies stems from a combination of strong feedback and a porous interstellar medium, which obscure any unique signatures that runaway stars provide.

brief.tldr: [research paper] INFERNO introduces a star-by-star hydrodynamic model that resolves individual stellar feedback events, including supernovae and runaway stars, in dwarf galaxy simulations. It demonstrates that galactic winds are driven by strong feedback and a porous interstellar medium, resulting in multi-phase outflows with distinct cold (low temperature, low velocity) and hot (high temperature, high velocity) components, and finds that runaway stars have negligible impact due to their location in diffuse gas and the dominance of feedback efficiency over their injection dynamics.

source_fields.title: INFERNO: Galactic winds in dwarf galaxies with star-by-star simulations including runaway stars

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: Including stellar feedback significantly reduces the star formation rate in dwarf galaxy simulations by depleting cold gas, with feedback models showing similar gas density distributions and a strong suppression of star formation compared to the no-feedback case, as evidenced by a time-averaged star formation rate below the observed value in WLM and consistent pressure and density profiles across models with natal stellar kinematics. Discussion: The INFERNO model reveals that dwarf galaxies produce strong, metal-enriched, multi-phase outflows with mass and metal loading factors >10 and energy loading near unity, in contrast to previous models that report energy loading factors around 0.1, highlighting the importance of stellar feedback efficiency and the limited role of natal velocities in regulating outflow properties. Introduction: This paper introduces a star-by-star feedback model that resolves the locality of supernova injections at parsec scales, enabling a more accurate representation of stellar feedback, chemical mixing, and galactic outflows by tracking individual stars rather than using single stellar populations. The INFERNO model: The method proposes a high-resolution star formation model that stochastically samples individual stars from a Kroupa IMF using predefined mass bins and Poisson statistics, with feedback calculated for stars above a mass threshold of 2 M⊙, enabling detailed tracking of stellar dynamics and feedback while maintaining computational efficiency through careful mass sampling and delay of star formation in low-mass cells. Summary \\& Conclusions: The INFERNO model demonstrates that star-by-star stellar feedback drives strong, energetically dominant galactic winds in dwarf galaxies, producing mass and metal loading factors consistent with observations while showing little sensitivity to runaway star velocities. Appendix A All simulations: This section presents simulations exploring how stellar natal velocity distributions—via stirring or kicks—affect feedback in dwarf galaxies, finding minimal impact on overall feedback rates but a notable increase in out-of-disc supernovae with higher kick fractions. Appendix B Resolution tests: This appendix shows that while lower resolution simulations with adjusted star formation thresholds produce only minor differences in outflow rates and loading factors, the impact on the circumgalactic medium (CGM) remains uncertain and may significantly affect outflow predictions. Numerical setup and model implementation: INFERNO is a high-resolution star-by-star simulation method that uses an adaptive-mesh-refinement code to model galactic evolution by combining detailed cooling physics with collisionless particle dynamics, where refinement is driven by mass rather than thermal Jeans criteria to resolve turbulent support in the interstellar medium.

abstract: INFERNO treats early stellar kinematics to include phenomena such as walkaway and runaway stars.

In contrast to massive disc galaxies, we find a surprisingly weak impact of the early stellar kinematics, with runaway stars having little to no effect on our results, despite exploding in diffuse gas
We demonstrate that this weak impact in dwarf galaxies stems from a combination of strong feedback and a porous interstellar medium, which obscure any unique signatures that runaway stars provide.
title: INFERNO: Galactic winds in dwarf galaxies with star-by-star simulations including runaway stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract describe a star-by-star hydrodynamic simulation model (INFERNO) for dwarf galaxies. Mentions runaway stars as a modeled phenomenon but no observational data or real stellar catalog is presented.

papers.title: High Velocity Stars in SDSS/APOGEE DR17
papers.author_names: Fredi Quispe-Huaynasi Fernando Roig Devin J. McDonald Veronica Loaiza-Tacuri Steven R. Majewski Fabio C. Wanderley Katia Cunha Claudio B. Pereira Sten Hasselquist Simone Daflon

abstract.text: We report 23 stars having Galactocentric velocities larger than $450~\\\\mathrm{km\\\\,s}^{-1}$ in the final data release of the APOGEE survey. This sample was generated using space velocities derived by complementing the high quality radial velocities from the APOGEE project in Sloan Digital Sky Survey's Data Release 17 (DR17) with distances and proper motions from Gaia early Data Release 3 (eDR3). We analyze the observed kinematics and derived dynamics of these stars, considering different potential models for the Galaxy. We find that three stars could be unbound depending on the adopted potential, but in general all of the stars show typical kinematics of halo stars. The APOGEE DR17 spectroscopic results and Gaia eDR3 photometry are used to assess the stellar parameters and chemical properties of the stars. All of the stars belong to the red giant branch, and, in general, they follow the abundance pattern of typical halo stars. There are a few exceptions that would deserve further analysis through high-resolution spectroscopy. In particular, we identify a high velocity Carbon-Enhanced Metal-Poor (CEMP) star, with Galactocentric velocity of 482 km\\\\,s$^{-1}$. We do not confirm any hypervelocity star in the sample, but this result is very sensitive to the adopted distances, and less sensitive to the Galactic potential.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study identifies 23 high-velocity stars (Galactocentric velocities >450 km/s) using combined APOGEE DR17 radial velocities and Gaia eDR3 distances/proper motions. Kinematic analysis shows most stars exhibit typical halo star behavior, with three potentially unbound depending on the Galactic potential model; no confirmed hypervelocity stars are found, though the result is sensitive to distance assumptions. One star is identified as a Carbon-Enhanced Metal-Poor (CEMP) halo member with a velocity of 482 km/s, suggesting possible accretion origin or in-situ formation in the Galactic halo.

source_fields.title: High Velocity Stars in SDSS/APOGEE DR17

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This study combines Gaia eDR3 astrometric and photometric data with APOGEE DR17 chemical and radial velocity data to identify and characterize high-velocity stars, leveraging Gaia's precise astrometry and APOGEE's detailed spectroscopic measurements for radial velocities and chemical abundances. Discussion: The HiVel stars are predominantly low-$\\alpha$ and low-Mg, consistent with an accreted origin, and their kinematic, chemical, and dynamical properties rule out origins in the Galactic disk, Galactic center, or Large Magellanic Cloud, with most falling into the accreted population defined by APOGEE DR17 data. Conclusions: This study identifies a sample of high-velocity stars, most consistent with halo or accreted stars from dwarf galaxy mergers, with no confirmed hypervelocity stars, and suggests their high velocities likely stem from dynamical interactions during galactic mergers rather than the Hills mechanism. Introduction: This paper introduces a unified framework for classifying and understanding high-velocity stars by linking their kinematic properties to specific astrophysical origins—such as binary interactions, galactic center dynamics, globular clusters, and minor mergers—thereby solving the problem of disentangling their formation mechanisms and leveraging them as sensitive tracers of the Milky Way's gravitational potential and structural evolution. Chemical Patterns: This section uses chemical tagging with APOGEE DR17 abundance data to show that most HiVel stars are metal-poor and alpha-enriched, resembling halo stars, with unbound candidates clearly falling within the halo population. Stellar parameters: The HiVel sample stars are predominantly located on the Red Giant Branch, with their stellar parameters—such as temperature, gravity, and metallicity—determined from Gaia and APOGEE data and compared to PARSEC isochrones. Appendix A Distances: This section evaluates and compares photogeometric distance estimates for HiVel stars from Bailer-Jones, StarHorse2, AstroNN, and Gaia parallaxes, finding that StarHorse2 distances are generally consistent with Bailer-Jones and critical for correctly identifying bound versus unbound hypervelocity stars. High velocity stars selection: This section identifies and characterizes high-velocity stars, including those exceeding the galactic escape velocity, using Gaia and APOGEE data to derive their positions, distances, proper motions, radial velocities, and estimated Galactocentric velocities. Appendix C Action diamond space: HiVel stars are predominantly found in the action diamond space region characteristic of radial orbits, with several falling within the green box indicating a possible origin from the Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus dwarf galaxy merger. Kinematics and orbit integrations: This section analyzes the spatial distribution and kinematics of High Velocity stars, concluding that they exhibit halo-like kinematics with both prograde and retrograde motions, and uses orbit integrations in the Irrgang Model I potential to confirm their dynamical behavior and trace their possible origins. Appendix B Model I Gravitational potential: The paper proposes a galactic gravitational potential model (Model I) that combines a Plummer bulge, a Miyamoto-Nagai disk, and a generalized spherical halo to describe the gravitational field of the Milky Way, with the core idea of accurately representing each component's structure using empirical parameterizations derived from observational data.

abstract: We report 23 stars having Galactocentric velocities larger than $450~\\\\mathrm{km\\\\,s}^{-1}$ in the final data release of the APOGEE survey.
We analyze the observed kinematics and derived dynamics of these stars, considering different potential models for the Galaxy.
We find that three stars could be unbound depending on the adopted potential, but in general all of the stars show typical kinematics of halo stars.
The APOGEE DR17 spectroscopic results and Gaia eDR3 photometry are used to assess the stellar parameters and chemical properties of the stars.
All of the stars belong to the red giant branch, and, in general, they follow the abundance pattern of typical halo stars.
title: High Velocity Stars in SDSS/APOGEE DR17

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title explicitly states 'High Velocity Stars in SDSS/APOGEE DR17'. papers.title: Hyper-runaway and hypervelocity white dwarf candidates in \\\\textit{Gaia} Data Release 3: possible remnants from Ia/Iax supernova explosions or dynamical encounters
papers.author_names: Andrei P. Igoshev Hagai Perets Na'ama Hallakoun

abstract.text: Type Ia and other peculiar supernovae (SNe) are thought to originate from the thermonuclear explosions of white dwarfs (WDs). Some of the proposed channels involve the ejection of a partly exploded WD (e.g. Iax SN remnant) or the companion of an exploding WD at extremely high velocities ($>400$ km\\\\,s$^{-1}$). Characterisation of such hyper-runaway/hypervelocity (HVS) WDs might therefore shed light on the physics and origins of SNe. Here we analyse the \\\\textit{Gaia} DR3 data to search for HVS WDs candidates, and peculiar sub-main-sequence (sub-MS) objects. We retrieve the previously identified HVSs, and find 46 new HVS candidates. Among these we identify two new unbound WDs and two new unbound sub-MS candidates. The remaining stars are hyper-runaway WDs and hyper-runaway sub-MS stars. The numbers and properties of the HVS WD and sub-MS candidates suggest that extreme velocity ejections ($>1000$ km\\\\,s$^{-1}$) can accompany at most a small fraction of type Ia SNe, disfavouring a significant contribution of the D6-scenario to the origin of Ia SNe. The rate of HVS ejections following the hybrid WD reverse-detonation channel could be consistent with the identified HVSs. The numbers of lower-velocity HVS WDs could be consistent with type Iax SNe origin and/or contribution from dynamical encounters. We also searched for HVS WDs related to known SN remnants, but identified only one such candidate.

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Analysis: This section describes the selection and analysis of rapidly moving white dwarf candidates from Gaia DR3, using strict astrometric and photometric criteria to identify potential hyper-runaway stars with velocities exceeding 400 km/s. Discussion: The main experimental finding is that only three hypervelocity white dwarfs (with velocities >1000 km s⁻¹) are consistent with double-degenerate dynamical detonation scenarios, which occur at a rate at least two orders of magnitude lower than the expected SN Ia rate, suggesting these events are rare and not a dominant source of hyper-runaway WDs. Conclusions: This study identifies 46 new candidate hyper-runaway and hypervelocity white dwarfs, primarily from the Galactic disc, supporting type Iax SNe or rare dynamical encounters as ejection mechanisms while disfavoring the double-detonation D6 model for normal type Ia SNe. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to constrain the origins of type Ia supernovae by identifying and analyzing hyper-runaway white dwarfs in the Gaia database, linking their properties to binary evolution and dynamical ejection scenarios that differ in their predicted rates and characteristics. Appendix A ADQL request: The section presents two ADQL queries to select high-quality Gaia DR3 source candidates based on astrometric and photometric criteria, with the first being more restrictive by including a brightness condition and the second being more relaxed. Previously characterised HVS WD candidates: This section reviews previously identified hypervelocity white dwarf candidates, highlighting their velocity properties, astrometric quality, and alternative explanations, while noting that most fail to meet the study's velocity or quality thresholds. Search for possible white dwarf–supernova remnant association: This study identifies Gaia DR3 5863122429179888000 as a potential hyper-runaway white dwarf associated with SNR G309.8+00.0 or G310.8-00.4, based on its proximity, motion, and kinematic constraints, while ruling out other candidates due to distance, age, or known associations. Objects of special interest and prime targets for follow-up characterisation: This section identifies four new hypervelocity white dwarf candidates with velocities exceeding 700 km s⁻¹, likely unbound from the Galaxy and requiring significant kicks, as prime targets for follow-up observations to determine their origins—whether from supernova explosions or dynamical interactions—while also highlighting bound or marginally-bound candidates that may represent the extreme end of halo populations or disc-origin hyper-runaways with peculiar properties. Appendix B A posterior estimate for the transversal velocity and credible intervals: This section presents a Bayesian approach to estimate the transversal velocity and its credible intervals, addressing the limitations of symmetric error propagation by using a normal prior for velocity components to avoid the Lutz-Kelker bias and produce a physically more reasonable velocity distribution.

abstract: Characterisation of such hyper-runaway/hypervelocity (HVS) WDs might therefore shed light on the physics and origins of SNe.
The remaining stars are hyper-runaway WDs and hyper-runaway sub-MS stars.
title: Hyper-runaway and hypervelocity white dwarf candidates in \\\\textit{Gaia} Data Release 3: possible remnants
